coredata.dc:title: CRISPR/Cas12a-derived ratiometric fluorescence sensor for high-sensitive Pb2+ detection based on CDs@ZIF-8 and DNAzyme
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Chuanyu
Du, Caiyi
Yuan, Feiyu
Yu, Peitong
Wang, Boxu
Su, Changshun
Zou, Ruiqi
Wang, Junyang
Yan, Xu
Sun, Chunyan
Li, Hongxia
coredata.dc:description: Benefiting from specific target recognition and trans-cleavage capabilities, the CRISPR/Cas12a system has great application prospects in the design of highly sensitive and rapid fluorescence biosensors. The CRISPR/Cas12a-based fluorophore-quencher molecular beacons exhibit single-color emission and are easily exposed to interference from environmental factors. Herein, we design a CRISPR/Cas12a-derived ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Pb2+ detection based on embedded carbon dots@zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (CDs@ZIF-8) composites and DNAzyme. The functions of ZIF-8 about encapsulating red emissive CDs in the inner cavity and adsorbing DNA on the outer surface are integrated to establish dual fluorescence signals, thereby reducing the possibility of interference and improving sensing accuracy. The presence of Pb2+ is converted into the change of activator by the GR5 DNAzyme to activate the CRISPR/Cas12a system, which provides signal amplification through multiple turnovers of side branch cutting, achieving highly sensitive detection of Pb2+ with a low detection limit of 18 pM. This method has the advantages of simplicity, universality, and excellent quantitative ability, and has broad prospects in sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CRISPR/Cas
Zeolitic imidazolate framework
Lead detection
Ratiometric fluorescence
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and principle study of CRISPR/Cas12a-CDs@ZIF-8 assay

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province ( 20230402024 GH), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 62371204 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing ( CSTB2023NSCQ-MSX0895 and cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0927 ), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2023T160263 ), the On-budget Capital Construction Fund of Jilin Province Development and Reform Commission ( 2023C014 ), the Graduate Innovation Fund of Jilin University ( 2023CX138 ), and the Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Jilin University ( S202310183342 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthetic procedure of CDs@ZIF-8. (B) TEM image of CDs. (C) High-resolution TEM image of CDs. (D) TEM image of CDs@ZIF-8. (E) Zeta potentials of ZIF-8, CDs, and CDs@ZIF-8. (F) The absorption spectra of ZIF-8, CDs, and CDs@ZIF-8. (G) FTIR spectra of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8.
Schematic illustration of the CRISPR/Cas12a-derived ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Pb2+ detection based on CDs@ZIF-8 and DNAzyme.

(A) Design of DNAzyme, activator, and crRNA. (B) Gel electrophoresis analysis of the feasibility of DNAzyme regulated CRISPR-Cas12a sensor. (C) Fluorescence spectra of the CRISPR-Cas12a sensor based on DNAzyme and CDs@ZIF-8.

Influence of different concentrations of GR5E (A), different reaction times (B), different concentrations of CDs@ZIF-8 (C), and different concentrations of GR5S-2 (D). F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of solutions with and without Pb2+. Error bars were calculated based on three replicate measurements.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CRISPR/Cas12a-CDs@ZIF-8 system in the presence of different concentrations of Pb2+. (B) The linear relationship between fluorescent ratio and concentration of Pb2+. (C) Specificity of the sensing system toward metal ions. (D) Anti-interference of the sensing system toward interfering substances. Error bars showed the standard deviations of three replicate measurements.
Fluorescent assay and ICP-MS for the detection of Pb2+ in tap water, river water, and fish samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: CRISPR/Cas12a-derived ratiometric fluorescence sensor for high-sensitive Pb2+ detection based on CDs@ZIF-8 and DNAzyme

College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Benefiting from specific target recognition and trans-cleavage capabilities, the CRISPR/Cas12a system has great application prospects in the design of highly sensitive and rapid fluorescence biosensors. The CRISPR/Cas12a-based fluorophore-quencher molecular beacons exhibit single-color emission and are easily exposed to interference from environmental factors. Herein, we design a CRISPR/Cas12a-derived ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Pb2+ detection based on embedded carbon dots@zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (CDs@ZIF-8) composites and DNAzyme. The functions of ZIF-8 about encapsulating red emissive CDs in the inner cavity and adsorbing DNA on the outer surface are integrated to establish dual fluorescence signals, thereby reducing the possibility of interference and improving sensing accuracy. The presence of Pb2+ is converted into the change of activator by the GR5 DNAzyme to activate the CRISPR/Cas12a system, which provides signal amplification through multiple turnovers of side branch cutting, achieving highly sensitive detection of Pb2+ with a low detection limit of 18 pM. This method has the advantages of simplicity, universality, and excellent quantitative ability, and has broad prospects in sensing applications.

Schematic illustration of the CRISPR/Cas12a-derived ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Pb2+ detection based on CDs@ZIF-8 and DNAzyme.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of toxic metal contaminant with bioaccumulation, lead ions (Pb2+) pose a great threat to human health (Ma et al., 2022). Even small amounts of Pb2+ can cause irreversible harm to nerve and immune systems (Yang et al., 2022b). To safeguard human health against potential exposure, standards and guidelines have been established to limit the presence of lead. The World Health Organization (WHO) and the USA Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) strictly limit the maximum lead level in drinking water to 10 ppb or 15 ppb (Jain et al., 2023). Therefore, highly sensitive methods for detecting Pb2+ are critically needed to ensure environmental and food safety. The traditional methods for Pb2+ detection included atomic absorption spectroscopy (Ahmed et al., 2023), atomic emission spectroscopy (Wensing et al., 1994), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (Zhang et al., 2018), and X-ray fluorescence spectrometry (Tighe et al., 2020). Although these methods have high sensitivity and specificity, they still have the disadvantages of complex instruments, long processing times, and high costs (Ihde et al., 2022). Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple, fast, and low-cost method for detecting Pb2+ in food and environment with high sensitivity and specificity. In the past few years, the utilization of functional nucleic acids as molecular recognition elements for the detection of heavy metals has become a research focus. Among them, DNAzyme exhibits excellent properties including high stability, high catalytic activity, simple synthesis, low cost, and anti-interference (Liang et al., 2024). The presence of Pb2+ activates DNAzyme, causing the substrate strand to cleave at the ribose-adenine (rA) site into two short strands (Liu et al., 2023). The integration of DNAzyme with other nucleic acid signal amplification techniques enables the establishment of highly sensitive and specific sensing platforms.

In recent years, clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR)/CRISPR-associated (Cas) systems have not only been applied to genome editing but also shown great potential in the field of biosensing for nucleic acid and non-nucleic acid targets because of their remarkable signal amplification efficiency (Kellner et al., 2019; Liang et al., 2019; Gootenberg et al., 2018; Tian et al., 2021). Among them, CRISPR/Cas12a employs a single CRISPR RNA (crRNA) as the guide RNA (gRNA) and possesses the capability to cleave single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) without the need for the protospacer adjacent motif (PAM) sequence (Zhang et al., 2022). Based on this property, various analytical methods for detecting trace amounts of non-nucleic acid targets have been established using functional nucleic acids (aptamer and DNAzyme) as molecular recognition elements (Samanta et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2022). These electrochemical, colorimetric, fluorometric, and other analysis methods employ distinct signal output designs. For electrochemical sensors, a common approach is to immobilize one end of a short DNA on the electrode surface, while the other end is modified with a signal probe such as ferrocene (Fc) (Ma et al., 2023) or methylene blue (MB) (Dai et al., 2019). After cleaving DNA, these probes move away from the electrode, triggering an electrochemical signal response. Regarding colorimetric sensors, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) (Choi et al., 2021) or horseradish peroxidase (HRP) (Samanta et al., 2022) are usually connected to DNA to achieve color changes. However, the above designs all require complex coupling and immobilization steps. Fluorescence sensors often convert unlocked nuclease activity into measurable fluorescent signals through the cleavage of molecular beacons, which are fluorophore-quencher pairs linked by short ssDNA (Chen et al., 2018; Green et al., 2022). This reporter exhibits single-color emission and is easily exposed to interferences from complex matrices. In addition, these methodologies including CRISPR-mediated readout and signal amplification require a series of complex manual procedures, which have a high risk of assay variation and erroneous results owing to human error, enzymatic degradation, and cross-contamination (Yan et al., 2023). While a one-pot assay serves as a valuable measure to mitigate contamination risks, it may not be sufficient as the sole safeguard against such events (Lin et al., 2022). Utilizing dual fluorescent signals to establish a ratiometric biosensor can avoid errors from receptor concentration, environmental factors, and instrumental variations. Ratiometric fluorescence reduces the occurrence of false positives and enhances the accuracy and reliability of detecting results owing to its self-calibration against background signal interference (Afshari et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2020b).

In this study, we designed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the detection of Pb2+, which is engineered by CRISPR/Cas12a and metal-organic framework (MOF) fluorescent nanocomposite. The internal cavity of MOF can be loaded with luminescent materials such as carbon dots (CDs), attributed to the flexible structure and tunable porosity of MOF (Yan et al., 2022). Furthermore, MOFs provide a large interface for DNA adsorption due to the high surface areas. Among them, ZIF-8 not only displays good quenching properties for fluorescent probes but also has a higher affinity for long ssDNA than short ssDNA (Yang et al., 2022a). ZIF-8 has the advantages of simple preparation, room temperature synthesis, low toxicity, and chemical stability. It is considered to be one of the most promising representatives of MOFs, which is beneficial to the construction of simple and efficient fluorescence sensors (Wang et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2023; Troyano et al., 2019). Using DNAzyme as the specific molecular recognition element, we designed sequences that link up with CRISPR/Cas12a systems. In the presence of Pb2+, the enzyme strand of GR5 DNAzyme binds to the crRNA and activates the Cas12a enzyme, which then shows nonspecific endonuclease activity toward ssDNA. Consequently, FAM-labeled ssDNA adsorbed by embedded carbon dots@zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (CDs@ZIF-8) composites can be degraded by the active Cas12a, thereby liberating ssDNA-FAM into solution. The fluorescence of CDs@ZIF-8 remained stable with or without Pb2+, therefore it can be used as an inner reference signal. This proposed ratiometric fluorescence method does not require complex covalent immobilization steps and can achieve accurate and highly sensitive detection of Pb2+ in environmental and food samples.

EnGen® Lba Cas12a (Cpf1) and NEBuffer™ r2.1 were from New England Biolabs (Ipswich, USA). Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 2-methylimidazole, and the standard solutions of Pb2+ and other metal ions were purchased from Aladdin Reagents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methanol (MeOH) was acquired from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (Massachusetts, USA). The standard reference materials for lead composition analysis (GBW09140, GBW09105) were purchased from the National Institute for Occupational Health and Poison Control, Chinese Center for Disease Control and Prevention (Beijing, China). Tris-HCl buffer, NaOH, NaCl, MgCl2, 10 × TBE, 4S GelRed, and dimethyl Acryl/Bis 40% Solution (29:1) were produced by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The 6 × loading buffer for gel electrophoresis was obtained from Takara Bio Co., (Dalian, China). The oligonucleotide samples (Table S1) were synthesized and purified by HPLC from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Milli-Q water was used to prepare all the solutions. The tips and tubes were RNase-free.

The fluorescence spectra were measured through the RF-5301 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan) at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm, with the slits of excitation and emission at 10 nm and 15 nm. The gel electrophoreses were obtained using the PowerPac HC electrophoresis system (Bio-Rad, California, America) and GelDoc XR + gel imaging system (Bio-Rad, USA). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected on the JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The absorption spectra were found by the 2550 UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). Zeta potentials were carried out on the Zetasizer Nano-ZS Nanoparticle Size Potentiometer (Malvern, UK). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using the spectrometer IRPrestige-21 (Shimadzu, Japan).

CDs were gifts from Prof. Bai Yang (Jilin University, Changchun, China), which were synthesized according to previously reported methods (Liu et al., 2020a). The ZIF-8 was synthesized according to the previous report (Yang et al., 2022a). For the synthesis of CDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites, a solution of CDs (5 mg/mL) in MeOH (1 mL) was added into MeOH (7.5 mL) containing Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (25 mM). Then, 2-methylimidazole (25 mM) in MeOH (7.5 mL) was added into the above solution and kept reaction for 12 h at room temperature under stirring. The CDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites were collected by centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 5 min), washed three times with MeOH, and vacuum-dried for 12 h.

Preparation of solution A: 3 μL of GR5E (30 nM) and 3 μL of GR5S-2 (30 nM) were mixed, kept at 95 °C for 5 min and slowly annealed to 25 °C. Then, 3 μL different concentrations of Pb2+ solutions were added to the mixture and incubated for 10 min at 37 °C.
Preparation of solution B: 1 μM Lba Cas12a (1 μL), crRNA-2 (300 nM, 3 μL), 10 × NEBuffer™ r2.1 (3 μL), and 11 μL Nuclease-free water were assembled to pre-incubate for 10 min at 25 °C.

Then, solution A and solution B were mixed for 10 min at 37 °C. 3 μL of ssDNA-FAM (5 μM) was added to the mixture for 30 min at 37 °C. Next, the solution was heated to 65 °C for 10 min to inactivate Cas12a. Subsequently, 100 μL buffer solution (50 mM Tris-HCl buffer containing 70 mM NaCl and 5 mM MgCl2) and 50 μL of CDs@ZIF-8 (30 μg/mL) were added and reacted at 25 °C for 10 min. Finally, water was added to the above mixture to reach a total volume of 500 μL, and the fluorescence spectra were collected. All experiments were performed and analyzed in three replicates.

12% non-denatured polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) was applied to verify the cleavage of ssDNA-FAM by Cas12a. 10 μL DNA sample and 2 μL loading buffer were mixed and loaded into the sample hole. The PAGE was performed in 1 × TBE running buffer at 110 V for 120 min. Finally, the gel was stained with 4S GelRed for 15 min and scanned using the GelDoc XR + gel imaging system.

Tap water, Songhua River water, fish samples (yellow croaker), and standard materials (freeze-dried bovine blood and human urine) were selected as real samples to assess the practical applicability of the fluorescence assay. In the recovery experiments, water samples were spiked with varying concentrations of Pb2+ and subsequently filtered using a 0.22 μm water filter membrane. The fish samples were processed in accordance with the National Standard for Food Safety of China (GB 5009.268-2016). In the digestion process for the fish samples, 0.5 g of the fish sample was combined with 7 mL of HNO3, 1 mL of hydrofluoric acid, 1 mL of H2O2, and various concentrations of Pb2+ standard solutions within the digestion vessel designed for microwave-assisted digestion. Subsequently, the excess HNO3 was eliminated by heating on a hot plate. The solution was then diluted to a final volume of 50 mL using ultra-pure water, with subsequent adjustment of the pH to 7.0. For comparison, Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry (ICP-MS) analysis was performed by the National Standard for Food Safety of China (GB 5009.268-2016) to evaluate the accuracy and reliability of the fluorescence biosensor. The standard materials were redissolved in water and diluted 1000 times for fluorescence detection.

CDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites are prepared by the simple coprecipitation approach via mixing Zn2+, CDs, and 2-methylimidazole in methanol (Fig. 1 A). The red emissive CDs made from taxus leaves exhibited a dot-shape with an average diameter of 0.81 ± 0.35 nm quantified by TEM image (Fig. 1B). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image mapped the obvious lattices in the carbon cores of CDs, with the lattice spacing of 0.17 and 0.18 nm (Fig. 1C). After encapsulating negatively charged CDs into the framework structure, CDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites possessed a typical hexagonal shape with average sizes of 305 ± 21 nm (Fig. 1D and E), similar in shape and size to pure ZIF-8 (Fig. S1). Moreover, CDs had wide-range absorption from UV to deep red light region, and the absorption peaks at 410 and 665 nm of CDs@ZIF-8 illustrated that CDs were encapsulated by ZIF-8 (Fig. 1F). More evidence was performed by the FTIR spectra of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. 1G). The absorption peaks at 3136 cm−1 and 2928 cm−1 showed the stretching vibration of the imidazole ring and the C–H in the methyl group, respectively. Furthermore, the peaks at 1146 cm−1, around 750 cm−1 (double bonds), and 422 cm−1 represented the vibration of C–N, imidazole ring, and Zn–N, respectively (Lao et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2022a). There was an additional peak at 1101 cm−1 of CDs@ZIF-8, which is attributed to the C–O–C vibration of CDs (Liu et al., 2020a). These results demonstrated that CDs were successfully encapsulated in ZIF-8 without destroying its key functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and principle study of CRISPR/Cas12a-CDs@ZIF-8 assay
ce:section.ce:para: The assay exploits the unique nucleolytic cleavage capability of the CRISPR/Cas12a system in conjunction with the luminescence and adsorption properties of CDs@ZIF-8, enabling the sensitive detection of Pb2+ (Fig. 2 ). The crRNA was designed to hybridize with the partial enzyme strand of GR5 DNAzyme (GR5E) to form a duplex. In the absence of Pb2+, the substrate strand of GR5 DNAzyme (GR5S) can hybridize with GR5E, while cannot bind to the Cas12a-crRNA complex (denoted “locked activator”). Consequently, the cleavage activity of Cas12a cannot be activated, thereby preserving the integrity of the ssDNA-FAM reporter. On the contrary, GR5S can be split into two short parts at the RNA site (rA) and release GR5E in the presence of Pb2+. As a result, GR5E becomes an “open activator” and hybridizes with crRNA, which triggers the activation of Cas12a and leads to the cleavage of ssDNA-FAM. In this situation, the added ssDNA reporter can be cleaved into fragments, which are no longer adsorbed by CDs@ZIF-8, resulting in a significant increase in the fluorescent signal of FAM at 520 nm. The fluorescent emission peak of encapsulated CDs at 665 nm remains stable in the presence or absence of Pb2+, therefore it can be used as an inner reference signal. Like in many molecular beacon designs, ssDNA-FAM serves as both a substrate for nucleases and a fluorescent signal probe through the length-controlled interaction with CDs@ZIF-8. CDs@ZIF-8 also plays dual roles, including adsorbing long ssDNA on its surface to quench the fluorescence of FAM, and encapsulating CDs in its inner cavity to obtain stable self-luminescence. Based on the above design, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor is constructed to quantitatively detect Pb2+.

It is essential to design the sequence to achieve target-mediated CRISPR cleavage. As shown in Fig. S2A, we designed two different groups of crRNA and corresponding complementary activators, which are part of GR5S and GR5E, respectively. The targeting sequence of crRNA generally varies from 18 to 24 bases (Chen et al., 2022), so we added four random bases to GR5S. The feasibility of the two designs was confirmed by gel electrophoresis (Fig. S2B). It can be seen that after adding low concentration (2 nM) and high concentration (10 nM) of Pb2+, the band changes of the second group (lanes 5–8) were more obvious, indicating that a portion of GR5E was more suitable as an activator. The reason for the small change for GR5S to serve as an activator may be that the introduction of random sequences makes the complementary relationship more complex, and the addition of the target is not effective enough.

Subsequently, we explored the adsorption and fluorescence quenching effects of ssDNA-FAM and CDs@ZIF-8. Previous studies revealed that positively charged ZIF-8 mainly relies on electrostatic interactions to adsorb negatively charged single-stranded DNA (Xie et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. S3, the potential of CDs@ZIF-8 had only a small decrease compared to ZIF-8. After adding ssDNA, the potentials of both were significantly reduced, indicating the existence of electrostatic interaction. It is also confirmed that CDs@ZIF-8 can indeed quench the fluorescence of FAM as effectively as ZIF-8 (Fig. S4). The length and base composition of ssDNA-FAM are important factors that cause sensitive changes in fluorescent signals. Our previous study (Yang et al., 2022a) found that the fluorescence quenching rate of ZIF-8 on 18-base ssDNA was approximately 70% higher than that on 5-base ssDNA, indicating that length is the key to fluorescence response. Finally, the base number of ssDNA was selected to be 20 due to this conclusion and other related literature (Wu et al., 2023). For base composition, composing ssDNA with identical bases prevents unnecessary complementation. Among the bases A, T, C, and G, the consecutive T and C bases can interact with Hg2+ and Ag+ (Shi et al., 2023), affecting the specificity of the method. Since the fluorescence of FAM can be quenched by G-quadruplexes, G-rich sequences are not a good choice either (Su et al., 2019). Therefore, ssDNA-FAM was ultimately determined to be composed of 20 consecutive A bases.

Finally, the designed sequences and their interactions are shown in Fig. 3 A. The feasibility of the experimental principle was verified through gel electrophoresis (Fig. 3B) and fluorescence experiments (Fig. 3C). As illustrated in Fig. 3B, ssDNA-FAM (lane 1) was not digested when only Cas12a + crRNA-2 was added (lane 2). After Cas12a + crRNA-2 was incubated with GR5E, ssDNA-FAM was digested into random short fragments (lane 3). It confirmed that GR5E as the activator played a crucial role in activating the cleavage activity of Cas12a. Furthermore, the addition of GR5S-2 locked the activator so that no DNA cleavage occurred (lane 4). When Pb2+ was present, ssDNA-FAM was clearly cleaved (lane 5), and the generated random short fragments were similar to those in lane 3, demonstrating that the activator was successfully unlocked and released from the DNAzyme. The fluorescence experiment was studied by introducing CDs@ZIF-8 as both the fluorescence quencher and inner reference signal (Fig. 3C). CDs@ZIF-8 can effectively quench the fluorescence of ssDNA-FAM and its mixture with Cas12a + crRNA-2. In the presence of GR5E, a remarkable increase in fluorescence (FL) intensity was observed, indicating that the short fragments would not be adsorbed by CDs@ZIF-8 after the activated Cas12a cleaved ssDNA-FAM. Adding GR5S-2 could lock the activator, and the fluorescence of ssDNA-FAM was quenched. After binding to Pb2+, GR5 DNAzyme was activated and cleaved, causing the activator to open. Activated Cas12a regained the ability to cleave ssDNA, and the fluorescence of FAM was enhanced. The quantitative detection of Pb2+ can be achieved by monitoring the fluorescence change of FAM. The fluorescence of CDs@ZIF-8 can be used as a stable reference signal to increase the reliability of the sensor.

Several key conditions were optimized to improve sensing performance. The concentration of GR5E and the cleavage reaction time are key factors affecting the cleavage ability of the CRISPR/Cas12a system. Obvious cleavage can be seen in the electrophoresis band at the concentration of GR5E from 25 to 40 nM (Fig. 4 A), and the fluorescence intensity increased steadily and stabilized at 30 nM as the optimal concentration (Fig. S5). As the reaction time between ssDNA-FAM and the mixture of crRNA, Cas12a, and GR5E increased, the cleavage products became clearer. Finally, 30 min was chosen as the reaction time (Fig. 4B). The fluorescence of ssDNA-FAM was effectively quenched at high concentrations of CDs@ZIF-8. Fluorescence restoration occurred when ssDNA-FAM was cleaved by activated Cas12a, with the optimal CDs@ZIF-8 concentration being 30 μg/mL (Fig. 4C). The fluorescence enhancement induced by Pb2+ was maximum when GR5S-2 was added at a concentration of 30 nM (Fig. 4D). The targeting sequence length in the crRNA was designed to be 18–24 nt, with the 22-nt targeting sequence displaying the most effective cleavage (Fig. S6) and the highest response to Pb2+ addition (Fig. S7). Besides, CDs@ZIF-8 quenched the fluorescence of ssDNA-FAM and reached a steady state within 10 min (Fig. S8).

Under optimized conditions, quantitative analysis was performed by monitoring the fluorescent ratio (F 520/F 665) after adding different concentrations of Pb2+. As depicted in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity of FAM at 520 nm increased accordingly as the Pb2+ concentration rose from 0 to 2.0 nM, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8 at 665 nm remained unchanged. The working curve was constructed using the fluorescent ratio (F 520/F 665) plotted against the concentration of Pb2+, revealing a good linear relationship (Fig. 5B). The limit of detection (LOD) for Pb2+ was 18 pM, which was much lower than the maximum lead limit value set by WHO and EPA. Compared to previously reported DNAzyme-based assays, the developed ratiometric fluorescence sensor demonstrated an excellent LOD (Table S2). Such excellent sensitivity of this analytical method can be ascribed to the following characteristics: (i) Through GR5 DNAzyme as an excellent catalyst, the presence of Pb2+ is transformed into the nucleic acid signal to activate the CRISPR/Cas12a system, which provides signal amplification through multiple turnovers of side branch cutting. (ii) As an outstanding signal converter, CDs@ZIF-8 has high quenching ability and ssDNA length-dependent adsorption capacity to provide further sensitive fluorescent signal output for the cleavage activity of CRISPR/Cas12a.

The capacity for selective recognition plays a crucial role in ensuring the accuracy of target identification and minimizing cross-reactivity in the sensing strategy. In order to simulate the actual application environment, the effect of other metal ions was explored to evaluate its specificity. As displayed in Fig. 5C, F 520/F 665 did not change significantly when only other metal ions were added. In addition, in the case where Pb2+ coexists with other ions, the signal of the system also showed the same response to Pb2+. Common interfering substances in water or fish samples also had little impact on the detection of Pb2+ (Fig. 5D), proving that the CRISPR/Cas12a-CDs@ZIF-8 platform possesses highly selective analysis of Pb2+ in complex samples. This good selectivity and specificity were attributed to the double specific recognition through both GR5 DNAzyme and the RNA-guided CRISPR-Cas12a system (Huang et al., 2019; Dmytrenko et al., 2023).

Accurate monitoring of Pb2+ in real samples is very important to assess its practical applicability. Three kinds of real samples (tap water, Songhua River water, and fish) were selected as the model matrices for investigating the applicability of the fluorescence sensor through the standard addition method by spiking different concentrations of Pb2+. As shown in Table 1 , the obtained recoveries ranged from 87.7% to 114.0%, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 3.2%, which were consistent with the ICP-MS assay. Additionally, the standard materials (lead in freeze-dried bovine blood and human urine) were redissolved in water and diluted 1000 times for fluorescence detection. The measured Pb2+ concentration in bovine blood was 310 ± 23 μg/L, consistent with the reference value of 318 ± 12 μg/L obtained through graphite furnace-atomic absorption spectrometry (GF-AAS). Concurrently, the Pb2+ concentration in human urine was identified at 292 ± 6 μg/L, demonstrating concordance with the reference value of 304 ± 28 μg/L obtained through GF-AAS. Compared with conventional spectroscopic methods, this method is only slightly less accurate but has advantages in terms of sensitivity and sample volume (Table S3). These results indicate the applicability of the ratiometric fluorescence system for the reliable detection of Pb2+ in environmental, food, and biological samples. It is worth mentioning that multiple fluorescent signals can effectively mitigate the background interference originating from complex matrices and have broad application prospects in routine pollution monitoring (Yang et al., 2022c).

In summary, this study demonstrated a CRISPR/Cas12a-derived ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy for high-sensitive Pb2+ detection based on CDs@ZIF-8 and DNAzyme. The introduced CDs@ZIF-8 combines the advantages of CDs and ZIF-8, including the excellent optical properties of CDs and the high surface area and cavity structure of ZIF-8, resulting in high sensitivity and high reliability of the sensing platform. Red emitting CDs serve as stable references, providing self-calibration opportunities to minimize the effects of interferences and enhance accuracy. The signal accumulation function of CRISPR/Cas12a improves the sensitivity of the method, enabling the detection of Pb2+ on the order of pM. We envision that the design of this sensor can be expanded to combine CRISPR/Cas technology with the distinctive advantages of other MOFs, such as catalysis and conduction, to develop biosensors with varied benefits. This detection strategy has the advantages of simplicity, sensitivity, and accuracy, which will contribute to an improved understanding of the multiple advantages of MOF-derived nanocomposites and lead to a broad application in conjunction with functional nucleic acids.

Chuanyu Yang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Caiyi Du: Investigation, Methodology. Feiyu Yuan: Methodology. Peitong Yu: Methodology. Boxu Wang: Investigation. Changshun Su: Validation. Ruiqi Zou: Validation. Junyang Wang: Writing – review & editing. Xu Yan: Writing – review & editing. Chunyan Sun: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Hongxia Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province (20230402024GH), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62371204), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (CSTB2023NSCQ-MSX0895 and cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0927), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023T160263), the On-budget Capital Construction Fund of Jilin Province Development and Reform Commission (2023C014), the Graduate Innovation Fund of Jilin University (2023CX138), and the Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Jilin University (S202310183342).
